fallback_for_note: GTA V uses RAGE 5.x. Red Dead Redemption 2 uses a later iteration — use this file as fallback only if no dedicated rdr2 engine file exists.

config_file_locations.windows_note: Single XML file contains all graphics and game settings. The Rockstar Games Launcher and in-game Graphics menu both rewrite this file — edits made while the game is running will be overwritten on exit.

keys.effect: Texture streaming quality: 0=normal, 1=high, 2=very high. Controls the resident texture pool size and streaming resolution.
keys.notes: VRAM-bound rather than GPU-compute-bound. High (1) is suitable for 6 GB VRAM; Very High (2) requires 8 GB+ for stable streaming.

keys.effect: Shader complexity for lighting and material rendering: 0=normal, 1=high, 2=very high.
Shadow map resolution and cascade count: 0=normal, 1=high, 2=very high, 3=ultra.
keys.notes: One of the highest GPU cost settings in GTA V. Dropping from Ultra to High recovers 10-20% GPU time in open-world areas.

keys.effect: Reflection probe and mirror quality: 0=normal, 1=high, 2=very high, 3=ultra.
keys.notes: Ultra reflection quality has a disproportionate VRAM cost with minimal visual benefit at typical play distances.
MSAA (2+) is extremely expensive in GTA V's open-world renderer. FXAA (1) is the best performance-to-quality option for most hardware. MSAA x4/x8 are only viable at 1080p on high-end hardware.

keys.effect: FXAA post-process anti-aliasing toggle: 0=off, 1=on. Applied on top of any MSAA setting.
keys.notes: Low GPU cost with meaningful edge-smoothing benefit. Recommend keeping on at all settings.

keys.effect: Grass density and draw distance: 0=normal, 1=high, 2=very high, 3=ultra.
keys.notes: Ultra grass quality causes significant GPU and CPU overhead in rural areas of San Andreas. Dropping to High is a reliable performance win with minimal visual change.
